Biochemistry: Concepts and Connections
Second Edition
Chapter 24
Carbohydrate Metabolism: Glycolysis, Gluconeogenesis, Glycogen Metabolism, and the Pentose Phosphate Pathway
Dean R. Appling
Spencer J. Anthony-Cahill
Christopher K. Mathews

Outline
24.1	D N A as the Template for R N A Synthesis
24.2	Enzymology of R N A Synthesis: R N A Polymerase
24.3	Mechanism of Transcription in Bacteria
24.4	Transcription in Eukaryotic Cells
24.5	Posttranscriptional Processing
24.6	Tools of Biochemistry
24.1 D N A as the Template for R N A Synthesis

Information Transfer in the Cell
D N A replication – D N A template-directed duplication of the genome prior to cell division
Transcription – D N A template-directed biosynthesis of R N A
Reverse transcription – R N A template-directed biosynthesis of D N A
Translation – m R N A template-directed biosynthesis of proteins

Regulation of Transcription by D N A- Protein Interactions
Gene expression is controlled by proteins that recognize particular D N A sequences and bind at those sites. These D N A-binding protein can either activate or repress the expression of certain (downstream) genes
Figure 24.1 Regulation of transcription by D N A–protein interactions.

The Operon Model (Jacob and Monod, 1961)
Figure 24.2 The operon model, as proposed in 1961 by Jacob and Monod
Bacterial genetics predicted messenger R N A to be a collection of metabolically active R N A s, present in low abundance, heterogeneous in size, and complementary to sequence regions in the D N A genome

Detection of m R N A Using Pulse Labeling and Sedimentation (1 of 3)
A centrifuge tube containing a sucrose gradient (5–20%) before (left) and after centrifugation (right) shows how R N A is separated by this technique
The S values refer to the sedimentation coefficients of the various particles in Svedberg units (S units depend on size and shape)
Figure 24.3 Demonstration of m R N A by pulse labeling and sedimentation.

Detection of m R N A Using Pulse Labeling and Sedimentation (2 of 3)
Figure 24.3 Demonstration of m R N A by pulse labeling and sedimentation.
(Left panel) Several prominent peaks (black trace) correspond to r R N A and t R N A; the radioactivity (red trace) is distributed heterogeneously  throughout, indicating a collection of m R N A s of various sizes

Detection of m R N A Using Pulse Labeling and Sedimentation (3 of 3)
Figure 24.3 Demonstration of m R N A by pulse labeling and sedimentation.
(Right panel) A short pulse (orange) highlights the existence of a variety of R N A species; continued growth in nonradiolabeled medium shows only the peaks corresponding the r R N A and t R N A; the signal of other R N A s has disappeared,
therefore m R N A s have a short half-life
24.2 Enzymology of R N A Synthesis: R N A Polymerase

Inhibitor Studies Reveal Three Distinct R N A Polymerases in Eukaryotes (1 of 2)
Treatment of bacteria with rifampicin blocks the synthesis of t R N A, r R N A, and m R N A, indicating bacteria contains one R N A polymerase

Inhibitor Studies Reveal Three Distinct R N A Polymerases in Eukaryotes (2 of 2)
Eukaryotes have three R N A polymerases, identified from their different sensitivities to α-amanitin (a toxin from the Amanita mushroom)

Escherichia coli R N A Polymerase Is Composed of Five Subunits
Table 24.1 Subunit composition of E coli R N A polymerase
aThe 70-k D  a  subunit is one of several alternative   subunits.

R N A Subunit Structures in the Three Domains of Life
Figure 24.4 R N A polymerase subunit structures in the three domains of life.
24.3 Mechanism of Transcription in Bacteria

Escherichia coli R N A Polymerase Holoenzyme Structure (1 of 3)
R N A polymerase holoenzyme has a molecular weight of about 450,000 D a
Figure 24.5 Crystal structure of R N A polymerase.

Escherichia coli R N A Polymerase Holoenzyme Structure (2 of 3)
Although R N A polymerase is active without σ-factor (red subunit), it binds to many more sites on the D N A than the holoenzyme
Figure 24.5 Crystal structure of R N A polymerase.

Escherichia coli R N A Polymerase Holoenzyme Structure (3 of 3)
σ-factor plays an important role in directing R N A polymerase to the promoter (site of initiation)
Figure 24.5 Crystal structure of R N A polymerase.

Cutaway View of Yeast R N A Polymerase Ⅱ
This view shows the structural features common to all multisubunit R N A polymerases
Figure 24.5 Crystal structure of R N A polymerase.

Conserved Promoter Sequences Recognized by Escherichia coli R N A Polymerase
The consensus sequence indicates the nucleotides most often found at each position
Figure 24.6 Conserved sequences in promoters recognized by E. coli R N A polymerase.

D N A Footprinting Analysis Identifies R N A Polymerase Binding Sites on D N A
Figure 24.7 Footprinting to identify binding sites on D N A for site-specific D N A-binding proteins.

Initiation of Transcription (1 of 2)
R N A polymerase first forms a closed-complex in which D N A is not unwound
Next, the stable, open complex forms where the −10 region is unwound
Figure 24.8 D N A bending in an open-promoter complex.

Initiation of Transcription (2 of 2)
Lastly, an
dependent isomerization further unwinds the D N A from −12 to +2
Figure 24.8 D N A bending in an open-promoter complex.

Elongation – The Transcription Bubble (1 of 2)
R N A chain growth begins by binding the template-specific r N T P, followed by binding of the second
Nucleophilic attack by the 3′-O H of the first nucleotide on the α-phosphate of the second nucleotide forms a phosphodiester bond in a manner analogous to D N A replication

Elongation – The Transcription Bubble (2 of 2)
As this process continues, the D N A is continuously unwound in a “transcription bubble”
σ-factor dissociates during incorporation of the first 10 nucleotides
Figure 24.9 The transcription bubble.

R N A Polymerase Advances Discontinuously, Often Pausing and Backtracking
The 3′-tail in the backtracked polymerase complex may be cleaved or move forward, repositioning the R N A 3′ nucleotide back into the active site
Pausing and backtracking control rate and fidelity
Figure 24.10 Backtracking in an elongation complex.

Termination of Transcription (1 of 3)
Termination of transcription in bacteria can occur in factor-independent and factor-dependent events
In factor-dependent termination, additional proteins (e.g., ρ protein and NusA) are involved in facilitating the release of the R N A transcript from R N A polymerase
Figure 24.11 A model for factor-independent termination of transcription.

Termination of Transcription (2 of 3)
In factor-independent termination, two symmetrical G C-rich segments form a stem-loop structure weakening the protein-D N A-R N A complex (G C rich segments slow transcription, allowing the stem-loop time to form)
Figure 24.11 A model for factor-independent termination of transcription.

Termination of Transcription (3 of 3)
The A-rich segment that follows further weakens these interactions, causing release of the transcript and termination of transcription
Figure 24.11 A model for factor-independent termination of transcription.
24.4 Transcription in Eukaryotic Cells

The Activity of the Homohexameric ρ Protein in Factor-Dependent Termination
Figure 24.12 Rho (ρ) factor–dependent termination.

Transcription in Eukaryotes Utilizes Three Polymerases Each Requiring Additional Protein Factors to Initiate Transcription
R N A polymerase I (pol I) – transcription of the major ribosomal R N A genes
R N A polymerase II (pol II) – transcription of protein-coding genes and some small R N A genes
R N A polymerase III (pol III) – transcription of small R N A genes
Transcription factors T F I, T F II, and T F III function with polymerases I, II, and III, respectively

Transcription and Processing of Ribosomal R N A by R N A Polymerase Ⅰ
Figure 24.13 Transcription and processing of the major ribosomal R N A s in eukaryotes.

T F IIIA Is a Zinc Finger Protein (1 of 2)
T F
is one of the
three transcription factors required by
pol
Zinc finger proteins form the structure (panel b)
The α-helix of zinc finger proteins fits within the major groove of D N A

T F IIIA Is a Zinc Finger Protein (2 of 2)
Zinc finger proteins are modular and may be strung together in series to bind to specific sequence regions of D N A (panel a)
Figure 24.14 Zinc fingers.

Structures of Some Eukaryotic Promoters
Figure 24.15 Structures of some eukaryotic promoters.
The T A T A box is the eukaryotic counterpart to the bacterial −10 region; additional regulatory sites may exist several kbp upstream from the initiation site, called enhancer regions

D N A Looping Brings Activator Proteins into Contact with Trans-Acting Factors and Other Transcription Factors (1 of 3)
Pol Ⅱ interacts with several transcription factors: TATA box-binding protein (T B P) and T F IIA, -B, -E, -F, and -H
T B P binds to D N A and bends it 90˚
Figure 24.17 A schematic representation of D N A looping as a process to bring enhancer-bound (E N H) activator (Act) proteins into contact with trans-acting factors (T A F s) associated with the core transcription complex.

D N A Looping Brings Activator Proteins into Contact with Trans-Acting Factors and Other Transcription Factors (2 of 3)
T F II B contains the B-linker element, which opens the D N A
Figure 24.17 A schematic representation of D N A looping as a process to bring enhancer-bound (E N H) activator (Act) proteins into contact with trans-acting factors (T A F s) associated with the core transcription complex.

D N A Looping Brings Activator Proteins into Contact with Trans-Acting Factors and Other Transcription Factors (3 of 3)
Communication between enhancer regions and proteins bound at the promoter proceed through the multiprotein complex called mediator
Figure 24.17 A schematic representation of D N A looping as a process to bring enhancer-bound (E N H) activator (Act) proteins into contact with trans-acting factors (T A F s) associated with the core transcription complex.

The R N A Polymerase Ⅱ Preinitiation Complex
Figure 24.16 Structure of the yeast R N A polymerase Ⅱ transcription preinitiation complex.

High Levels of Histone Acetylation Is Associated with High Transcriptional Activity
Figure 24.18 Acetylation of core histones.
Neutralization of the basic lysine residues via acetylation weakens the ionic interactions between histones and D N A in chromatin

Termination of Transcription in Eukaryotes (1 of 2)
R N A polymerase II usually transcribes well past the end of the gene, passing through one or more T T A T T T signals
The pre-m R N A carrying the A A U A A A signal is cleaved 11 to 30 residues downstream of these sites
Figure 24.19 Termination of transcription in eukaryotes: Addition of poly(A) tails.

Termination of Transcription in Eukaryotes (2 of 2)
A poly(A) tail is then added by poly(A) polymerase
Poly(A) tails relate to m R N A stability, the longer the tail, the longer the half-life
Figure 24.19 Termination of transcription in eukaryotes: Addition of poly(A) tails.
24.5 Posttranscriptional Processing

r R N A and t R N A Are Synthesized as Much Larger Transcripts That Undergo a Variety of Cleavage Events (1 of 2)
Figure 24.20 Structure of E. coli 30S pre-r R N A.
Pre-r R N A posttranscriptional processing of the E. coli 30S ribosome

r R N A and t R N A Are Synthesized as Much Larger Transcripts That Undergo a Variety of Cleavage Events (2 of 2)
Figure 24.21 Modification steps (1–4) that occur in the maturation of E. coli t R N ATyr from its transcript and modified bases (step 5) seen in the mature t R N A. The t R N A sequence is shown in blue.
Pre-t R N A posttranscriptional  processing

Eukaryotic Pre-m R N A Is Capped by 7-Methylguanosine at the 5′-end
Figure 24.22 Structure of a processed m R N A 5′ end.

After Capping, Pre-m R N A Undergoes Splicing (1 of 3)
In the splicing process small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (s n R N P s) such as U1 and U2 aid in pre-m R N A loop formation
Figure 24.24 A schematic view of the mechanism of m R N A splicing.

After Capping, Pre-m R N A Undergoes Splicing (2 of 3)
The 2′-O H of the branchpoint (in this case A) performs a nucleophilic attack on the phosphate of a base at the 5′ splice site (here, G) forming a lariat and releasing the 5′ exon
Figure 24.24 A schematic view of the mechanism of m R N A splicing.

After Capping, Pre-m R N A Undergoes Splicing (3 of 3)
The 3′-O H of the 5′ exon then attacks the phosphate of the first nucleotide of the 3′ exon forming the spliced m R N A
The s n R N P-pre–m R N A complex is called the spliceosome
Figure 24.24 A schematic view of the mechanism of m R N A splicing.

Structure of Human U1 Small Nuclear R N A (s n R N A) Together with Its Splicing Site
Figure 24.23 Structure of a small nuclear R N A (s n R N A).

Schematic View of the m R N A Splicing Mechanism
Figure 24.24 A schematic view of the mechanism of mRNA splicing.

Overall Process of Splicing
Figure 24.25 The overall process of splicing.

Seven Alternative Splicing Pathways in the α-Tropomyosin Gene
Figure 24.26 α-Tropomyosin gene organization (rat) and seven alternative splicing pathways.
Alternative splicing allows one gene to specify multiple proteins
24.6 Tools of Biochemistry

Genome-Wide Identification of D N A-Binding Proteins by Chromatin Immunoprecipitation
Figure 24B.1 Chromatin immunoprecipitation.

Chapter 24 Summary (1 of 3)
All classes of R N A are synthesized by the template-dependent copying of one D N A strand within a gene, catalyzed by R N A polymerase(s)
R N A polymerase(s) transcribe in 5′ → 3′ direction, using 5′-ribonucleotide triphosphates as substrates
Bacteria synthesize all classes of R N A with one polymerase, whereas eukaryotic cells have three different polymerases (I, II, and III) to produce ribosomal (r R N A), messenger (m R N A), and transfer (t R N A) R N A species, respectively

Chapter 24 Summary (2 of 3)
D N A strand selection and duplex unwinding and rewinding are carried by R N A polymerase(s) in conjunction with other proteins
R N A polymerases bind at promoter sites by formation of specific D N A-protein contacts to initiate and then elongate R N A synthesis
The process of R N A elongation is terminated by specific D N A sequences, sometimes facilitated by other proteins

Chapter 24 Summary (3 of 3)
Posttranscriptional processing includes trimming t R N A and r R N A precursors as well as (in eukaryotes) cutting and splicing m R N A precursors
While bacterial m R N A undergoes little processing, eukaryotic m R N A is extensively modified, with polyadenylylation at the 3′-end, capping at the 5′-end, and splicing throughout the gene
